articles.title: US and Iran agree to halt Strait of Hormuz attacks, set Doha talks as both sides 'stand down for now'
articles.summary: After a weekend of tit-for-tat strikes near the Strait of Hormuz, US and Iranian officials agreed to halt attacks and let vessels 'move freely,' with a US official saying both sides would 'stand down for now.' Washington said technical talks on managing shipping through the strait would resume in Doha, Qatar, with reports pointing to a Tuesday meeting, though Iran's Foreign Ministry disputed that any negotiating session was firmly scheduled and said mine clearance in the strait would be carried out solely by Iran under the Islamabad memorandum. Iran's foreign minister warned that any challenge to Tehran's control of the waterway, or attempts to impose 'separate arrangements,' would 'increase tensions.' Dozens of trade vessels transited the strait over the weekend despite the exchanges, with shippers still avoiding the Omani route after a tanker was hit.

articles.title: Iran strikes Bahrain and Kuwait after US attacks, threatens to end negotiations; Qatari citizen killed by shrapnel
articles.summary: Iran fired drones and missiles at targets in Bahrain and Kuwait in response to US strikes, damaging a residential building in Manama and prompting Gulf states to condemn the escalation as a test of the fragile US-Iran ceasefire. Qatar said one of its citizens was killed by shrapnel attributed to 'military operations' in the region. Tehran paired the attacks with a threat to walk away from negotiations, even as its foreign minister simultaneously called for a new security framework with Gulf countries. The strikes on two more Gulf states widened the conflict's footprint beyond the Iran-US-Israel axis and rattled energy markets, pushing oil prices higher.

articles.title: Iran's foreign minister calls for Gulf security framework as Tehran set to receive $6bn in frozen assets
articles.summary: Iran's foreign minister called for the establishment of a joint security framework with Gulf Arab states, an appeal made just hours after Iranian forces struck Bahrain and Kuwait, underscoring Tehran's attempt to pair coercion with diplomacy. Iranian state media said the country is set to receive about $6 billion in frozen assets held in Qatar as part of arrangements tied to the ceasefire. An adviser to Iran's supreme leader separately warned Oman against aligning with outside powers over the Strait of Hormuz, while Arab News reported that Europeans on the UN Security Council cautioned businesses over the legal and reputational risks of West Bank settlement deals. Gulf capitals remain wary, having borne direct costs of the conflict.

articles.title: Israel demolishes 200-metre Hezbollah tunnel and strikes south Lebanon command posts despite framework deal
articles.summary: The Israeli military said it demolished a Hezbollah tunnel stretching more than 200 metres and reaching over 25 metres deep, describing it as a drone facility that held hundreds of weapons and launch shafts, and struck Hezbollah command centres in the Nabatieh and Mayfadoun areas of southern Lebanon. Israeli officials said a classified annex to the new US-brokered framework deal preserves the IDF's 'freedom of action' inside its security zone and ties any withdrawal to conditions on the ground rather than a fixed timetable. The operations continued days after Israel and Lebanon signed the framework agreement in Washington, highlighting the gap between the diplomatic track and persistent strikes that Lebanon and Hezbollah call ceasefire violations.

articles.title: Israeli forces detonate explosives in south Lebanon village and strike Gaza's Deir el-Balah amid 'ceasefire'
articles.summary: A massive Israeli-triggered explosion shook the southern Lebanese village of Majdal Zoun, the latest in a series of demolitions and strikes that residents and Lebanese authorities describe as repeated violations of the truce. In Gaza, an Israeli strike hit Deir el-Balah, part of continuing attacks even as a ceasefire is nominally in force. Al Jazeera framed the incidents within a broader pattern in which overall violence has eased since the regional deals but Israel continues operations against frontline villages and what it calls militant infrastructure. Israel says it targets Hezbollah and Hamas assets; Lebanese and Palestinian officials say civilians and property bear the cost.

articles.title: Israeli forces raid Birzeit University in occupied West Bank, assault security guard
articles.summary: Israeli forces stormed Birzeit University near Ramallah early Monday, assaulting a security guard and damaging property in the Faculty of Physical Education before withdrawing, according to Middle East Monitor. The raid came amid a wider pattern of incursions across the occupied West Bank; Palestinian officials say that since 8 October 2023, Israeli military and settler attacks in the territory have killed at least 1,173 Palestinians, injured more than 12,600 and led to roughly 23,000 arrests. The Israeli military said separately that it opened fire on what it called rioters near Ramallah after a 'violent riot' involving stone-throwing, following Palestinian reports that a teenager had been killed.

articles.title: Palestinian UN envoy says 'time for condemning is over,' demands end to Israeli annexation
articles.summary: Palestinian UN envoy Riyad Mansour told the Security Council that 'annexation is an act of war' that 'kills all peace efforts,' saying Israel has effectively annexed over 60 percent of the West Bank and all of East Jerusalem and warning that settlement plans in the E1 area would be 'a death penalty for the Palestinian state.' His appeal came as European members of the Security Council warned businesses of legal and reputational risks tied to West Bank settlement dealings. The intervention reflects mounting Palestinian frustration that international statements have not translated into measures to halt land seizures, demolitions and settlement expansion.

articles.title: Druze leader warns of 'quiet massacre' of Druze in Syria's Sweida, urges Western action
articles.summary: Sheikh Mowafaq Tarif, spiritual leader of Israel's Druze community, called on the United States and Europe to halt what he described as a 'quiet massacre' of Druze in Sweida, southern Syria, saying thousands were killed in 2025 and hundreds kidnapped, with women and children among the victims. The appeal spotlights continued sectarian violence and instability in post-war Syria, where Damascus's new authorities are seeking international legitimacy; Syria's intelligence chief told the UN that the country had 'turned the page on extremism' while casting Israel as the principal remaining threat. Rights advocates have pressed for protection of minority communities as armed groups and local factions vie for control.
